category.label: Differentiation: Definition and Basic Rules
category.description: Develops the derivative as a limit of a difference quotient and builds the full toolkit of basic differentiation rules — power, constant, sum/difference, product, quotient, and the standard transcendental derivatives (sin, cos, e^x, ln x, tan, cot, sec, csc). Bridges the conceptual definition of derivative (slope of tangent, instantaneous rate of change) to fluent rule-based computation, including differentiability conditions and numerical/graphical estimation.
category.yield_rationale: Unit 2 is the computational engine of the entire exam — 10-12% unit weight, ~23 FRQ appearances, and every subsequent unit assumes fluent derivative computation; power/product/quotient rules appear implicitly in virtually every FRQ part.

umbrellas.label: Average and Instantaneous Rates of Change
umbrellas.description: Computes the average rate of change over an interval as the slope of a secant line (Δy/Δx), then motivates the instantaneous rate by taking the limit as the interval shrinks to zero.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Rate-of-change interpretation drives every contextual FRQ sub-part; tables of values require this skill every exam year (Archetype 1 FRQ).

in_depth.label: Average Rate of Change Formula on [a, b]
in_depth.description: Applies the formula (f(b) − f(a)) / (b − a) to compute the average rate of change of a function on a closed interval, recognizing this as the slope of the secant line through (a, f(a)) and (b, f(b)).
in_depth.yield_rationale: AROC computation from an equation or table is a direct sub-task in nearly every Rate/Data FRQ; tested in Part A and Part B.

in_depth.label: Average Rate of Change from a Table of Values
in_depth.description: Reads two function values from a table and computes (f(b) − f(a)) / (b − a), selecting the appropriate rows; recognizes that only the endpoint values are needed regardless of intermediate data.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Table-based AROC is the entry step for the Rate/Data FRQ archetype that appears every exam year.

in_depth.label: Slope of the Secant Line and Its Geometric Meaning
in_depth.description: Identifies the average rate of change as the slope of the secant line through two points on a curve and interprets the sign and magnitude of that slope in context.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conceptual link between AROC and secant slope is tested in MC justification questions; prerequisite for the limit definition of derivative.

in_depth.label: Instantaneous Rate of Change as a Limit
in_depth.description: Describes the instantaneous rate of change at x = a as lim_{h→0} [f(a+h) − f(a)] / h, connecting the shrinking-interval process to the slope of the tangent line.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conceptual definition links Unit 1 limit fluency to Unit 2; appears in MC questions that test whether students understand the derivative as a limit.

in_depth.label: Comparing Average Rate vs Instantaneous Rate
in_depth.description: Distinguishes between average rate of change (secant slope over an interval) and instantaneous rate (derivative at a point); identifies which quantity a given problem context is requesting.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conceptual distinction is tested in MC interpretation questions; a common distractor trap in contextual FRQ sub-parts.

umbrellas.label: Derivative as a Limit of a Difference Quotient
umbrellas.description: Formally defines the derivative using the limit definition f'(x) = lim_{h→0} [f(x+h) − f(x)] / h and the equivalent form f'(a) = lim_{x→a} [f(x) − f(a)] / (x − a); establishes derivative notation.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Limit-definition questions appear in MC every year; notation fluency is required on every derivative FRQ; used as a bridge skill to all downstream differentiation.

in_depth.label: Setting Up the Difference Quotient f(x+h) − f(x)) / h
in_depth.description: Substitutes x + h into a given function, forms the difference quotient, and simplifies algebraically before taking the limit; tests polynomial and rational function substitution accuracy.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Computing f(x+h) accurately (expanding binomials, canceling h) is a direct MC skill tested most years; prerequisite for every limit-definition derivative problem.

in_depth.label: Computing a Derivative via the Limit Definition
in_depth.description: Evaluates lim_{h→0} [f(x+h) − f(x)] / h by expanding, simplifying, canceling the h in the denominator, and taking the limit to obtain f'(x) or f'(a).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Full execution of limit-definition derivative is tested in no-calculator MC and occasionally in FRQ; measures algebraic manipulation fluency under the limit.

in_depth.label: Recognizing a Limit Expression as a Derivative
in_depth.description: Given a limit expression in difference-quotient form, identifies the function f and the point a (or x) so that the limit equals f'(a) or f'(x), without evaluating the derivative directly.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Matching a limit to a derivative is one of the highest-frequency MC problem types in Unit 2; tests notation fluency and structural recognition rather than computation.

in_depth.label: Interchanging Derivative Notation: f'(x), dy/dx, d/dx
in_depth.description: Reads and writes the derivative using prime notation f'(x), Leibniz notation dy/dx, and operator notation d/dx[f(x)]; interprets each at a point (e.g., f'(a) vs dy/dx|_{x=a}).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Notation fluency is required on every derivative question; FRQ answer justifications must use correct notation to earn communication points.

in_depth.label: Alternate Limit Form: lim_{x→a} [f(x) − f(a)] / (x − a)
in_depth.description: Uses the alternate difference-quotient form with x → a (instead of h → 0) to compute or recognize the derivative at a specific point; applies algebraic cancellation to resolve the 0/0 form.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Alternate limit form appears in MC; students must recognize both forms as the same derivative definition — a common source of errors on recognition questions.

umbrellas.label: Estimating Derivatives Numerically and Graphically
umbrellas.description: Approximates f'(a) by computing a difference quotient with a small h from a table of values, or by reading the slope of a tangent line from a graph; interprets the estimate in context.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Table-based derivative estimation is a standard FRQ sub-task in the Rate/Data archetype (e.g., 2024 Q1, 2023 Q3); appears in both calculator and no-calculator sections.

in_depth.label: Symmetric Difference Quotient for Derivative Estimation
in_depth.description: Estimates f'(a) using the symmetric difference quotient [f(a+h) − f(a−h)] / (2h) when both neighboring table values are available; recognizes this as a more accurate approximation than one-sided.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Symmetric difference quotient from a table is the standard FRQ estimation technique; graders award method points for using the closest symmetric pair.

in_depth.label: One-Sided Difference Quotient for Derivative Estimation
in_depth.description: Estimates f'(a) using a forward or backward difference quotient [f(a+h) − f(a)] / h when only one neighboring data point is available; identifies which direction is forced by the table.
in_depth.yield_rationale: One-sided estimation arises at endpoints of a table or when asked to interpret a specific sub-interval; tested in both MC and FRQ.

in_depth.label: Estimating f'(a) from a Graph Using Tangent Slope
in_depth.description: Reads the slope of the tangent line to a curve at x = a from a graph by identifying two clear points on the tangent or using the rise-over-run of the curve's local behavior.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graphical derivative estimation appears in MC (Part A) when a graph replaces analytic data; connects to the graphical representation of derivative value.

in_depth.label: Interpreting the Units and Meaning of an Estimated Derivative
in_depth.description: Attaches correct units (output units per input units) to a numerically estimated derivative and states what the value means in the applied context (e.g., 'the temperature is decreasing at 3 degrees per minute').
in_depth.yield_rationale: Unit interpretation and contextual meaning of the derivative are required in virtually every applied FRQ sub-part asking for an estimate; graders specifically check for correct units.

umbrellas.label: Differentiability and Continuity
umbrellas.description: Establishes that differentiability implies continuity (but not vice versa); identifies non-differentiable points — corners, cusps, vertical tangents, and discontinuities — by examining one-sided derivatives or the graph.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Differentiability appears in MC and FRQ justification tasks ('explain why f is not differentiable at x = a'); the FRQ justification demand makes this slightly more exam-relevant than AROC estimation; requires connecting Unit 1 continuity to Unit 2 derivative existence.

in_depth.label: Differentiability Implies Continuity (but Not Vice Versa)
in_depth.description: States and applies the theorem that if f is differentiable at x = a then f is continuous at x = a; provides or identifies a counterexample showing continuity does not guarantee differentiability (e.g., f(x) = |x| at 0).
in_depth.yield_rationale: This logical implication is a common MC justification type; distinguishing the two conditions is tested in FRQ explanations.

in_depth.label: Non-Differentiability at a Corner (One-Sided Derivatives Differ)
in_depth.description: Identifies a corner (sharp turn) in a graph as a non-differentiable point by showing the left-hand and right-hand derivatives exist but are not equal; uses absolute-value or piecewise functions as examples.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Corner non-differentiability is the most commonly tested non-differentiable point type in MC and FRQ; appears most frequently in piecewise-function and absolute-value contexts — the dominant case among the three geometric non-differentiability types.

in_depth.label: Non-Differentiability at a Cusp (Infinite One-Sided Derivatives)
in_depth.description: Identifies a cusp as a point where the one-sided derivatives approach +∞ and −∞ (or both ±∞), making the derivative undefined; distinguishes a cusp from a corner by the blow-up of the secant slopes.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Cusps appear less frequently than corners in AP problems but are part of the non-differentiability classification tested in MC; moderately lower priority than corner cases.

in_depth.label: Non-Differentiability at a Vertical Tangent
in_depth.description: Identifies a vertical tangent as a non-differentiable point because the limit of the difference quotient is ±∞ (derivative undefined); recognizes this graphically as an infinitely steep tangent line.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Vertical tangents are tested in MC least frequently among the three geometric non-differentiable point types; appear as a classification option but rarely as the sole focus of an exam question.

in_depth.label: Discontinuity Makes f Non-Differentiable
in_depth.description: Explains that any discontinuity (removable, jump, or infinite) at x = a makes f non-differentiable at a, since differentiability requires continuity as a prerequisite.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Connecting discontinuity to non-differentiability is a standard MC question type; students must apply the contrapositive of the differentiability-implies-continuity theorem.

umbrellas.label: Power Rule, Constant Rule, and Linearity of Differentiation
umbrellas.description: Applies d/dx(x^n) = nx^(n−1) for any real n (including negative and fractional exponents), the constant rule d/dx(c) = 0, and the sum/difference/constant-multiple rules to differentiate polynomial and power-function combinations.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Power rule and linearity rules are prerequisites for every differentiation computation on the exam; yield anchored near the spec's chain rule 0.95 benchmark given ~100% downstream use.

in_depth.label: Power Rule for Positive Integer Exponents
in_depth.description: Applies d/dx(x^n) = nx^(n−1) for positive integer n to differentiate monomials and recognizes the resulting power decrease by one.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Integer power rule is the single most-applied differentiation sub-skill on the exam; appears in every polynomial derivative computation.

in_depth.label: Power Rule for Negative Exponents (Reciprocal Functions)
in_depth.description: Rewrites f(x) = 1/x^n as x^(−n) and applies the power rule to obtain −n·x^(−n−1); requires fluency with negative-exponent rewriting before differentiating.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Negative-exponent power rule appears frequently in MC and in FRQ setup; a common error is forgetting to rewrite before applying the rule.

in_depth.label: Power Rule for Fractional Exponents (Radical Functions)
in_depth.description: Rewrites f(x) = x^(p/q) (from a radical or given form) and applies d/dx(x^(p/q)) = (p/q)·x^(p/q − 1); simplifies the resulting expression with a fractional exponent.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Fractional-exponent derivatives appear regularly in MC and are often embedded in larger FRQ expressions; rewriting radicals as powers is a required precalc prerequisite.

in_depth.label: Derivative of a Constant is Zero
in_depth.description: States and applies d/dx(c) = 0 for any constant c; recognizes that constant terms vanish in differentiation and does not confuse with the constant-multiple rule.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Constant-rule application is implicit in every polynomial derivative; errors here (treating a constant as a variable) are among the most common distractor traps.

in_depth.label: Constant Multiple Rule: d/dx[c·f(x)] = c·f'(x)
in_depth.description: Applies the constant-multiple rule to pull a scalar coefficient outside the derivative operator before applying the power or other rule.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Constant-multiple rule is used in virtually every differentiation problem involving coefficients; failure to apply it correctly is a high-frequency algebraic error.

in_depth.label: Sum and Difference Rule for Polynomials
in_depth.description: Differentiates a sum or difference of functions term-by-term using (f ± g)' = f' ± g', producing the full derivative of a polynomial or combined expression.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Sum/difference rule is applied in every polynomial differentiation task on the exam; foundational for all multi-term derivative computations.

in_depth.label: Rewriting the Expression (Radical/Rational) Before Differentiating
in_depth.description: Rewrites expressions with roots or reciprocals as power functions (e.g., √x = x^(1/2), 1/x² = x^(−2)) before differentiating; identifies when rewriting is necessary.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Rewriting is a required pre-step for ~30% of MC power-rule questions; tested as a combined skill with the rule itself.

umbrellas.label: Derivatives of sin(x), cos(x), e^x, and ln(x)
umbrellas.description: Applies and recalls the four fundamental transcendental derivative formulas — d/dx(sin x) = cos x, d/dx(cos x) = −sin x, d/dx(e^x) = e^x, d/dx(ln x) = 1/x — in isolation and combined with linearity rules.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Spec anchor: these four formulas appear on virtually every AP exam in both MC and FRQ; essential fluency for all downstream differentiation and integral computation.

in_depth.label: Derivative of sin(x): d/dx[sin x] = cos x
in_depth.description: Recalls the single formula d/dx(sin x) = cos x; applies it to differentiate expressions involving sin(x) multiplied by a constant or added to other terms, isolating the sine derivative rule as the only skill tested.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Sine derivative is tested in isolation on MC problems using only sine terms (e.g., 5sin x); appears in nearly every exam year in both sections.

in_depth.label: Derivative of cos(x): d/dx[cos x] = −sin x
in_depth.description: Recalls the single formula d/dx(cos x) = −sin x, including its negative sign; applies it to differentiate expressions involving cos(x) multiplied by a constant or added to other terms, with the negative sign as the primary precision target.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Cosine derivative's negative sign is the most common single-formula error on MC; isolating this rule lets problems specifically test whether the student applies the negative correctly.

in_depth.label: Derivative of e^x
in_depth.description: Recalls d/dx(e^x) = e^x; applies this self-referential derivative to expressions involving scalar multiples of e^x or e^x added to other functions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: d/dx(e^x) = e^x is the most memorable derivative formula; appears in MC and in DE/integration FRQ sub-parts as a foundational fact.

in_depth.label: Derivative of ln(x)
in_depth.description: Recalls d/dx(ln x) = 1/x for x > 0; applies this formula to differentiate expressions involving ln(x) with constant multiples, and distinguishes from the derivative of log base b.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Derivative of ln(x) appears in MC and FRQ sub-parts involving logarithmic functions; combines with chain rule in Unit 3 (U-sub in Unit 6).

in_depth.label: Combining Transcendental Derivatives with Sum/Constant-Multiple Rules
in_depth.description: Differentiates expressions like f(x) = 3sin(x) − 2e^x + 4ln(x) by applying linearity rules term-by-term across the four transcendental formulas.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Mixed transcendental expressions with constant multiples are a standard MC format; errors arise from misapplying the constant to the wrong formula.

umbrellas.label: The Product Rule
umbrellas.description: Applies the product rule (fg)' = f'g + fg' to differentiate a product of two differentiable functions; identifies which factor is f and which is g, then executes and simplifies the result.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Product rule appears in ~60% of derivative-heavy FRQ parts; tested explicitly in MC; prerequisite for chain-rule composites involving products in Unit 3.

in_depth.label: Identifying Factors and Setting Up the Product Rule
in_depth.description: Recognizes when an expression is a product of two distinct functions and writes the product rule template (fg)' = f'g + fg' with the correct assignments for f and g.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Correct identification of the product structure is the gateway step; errors in factor assignment lead to wrong-answer traps in MC.

in_depth.label: Product Rule: Polynomial × Trigonometric Function
in_depth.description: Applies the product rule to differentiate a product such as x²·sin(x) or (3x+1)·cos(x), combining polynomial and trig derivatives in a single expression.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Polynomial-times-trig products are the most common product rule MC test case; tests both polynomial and trig derivative recall simultaneously.

in_depth.label: Product Rule: Polynomial × Exponential Function
in_depth.description: Applies the product rule to differentiate a product such as x³·e^x or (2x−5)·e^x, combining power-rule and e^x derivatives.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Poly-times-exp products appear in MC and arise naturally in differential-equation setups (Unit 7); tests e^x derivative fluency under the product structure.

in_depth.label: Simplifying and Factoring the Product Rule Result
in_depth.description: Collects and simplifies the two terms f'g + fg' by factoring out common factors or combining like terms; recognizes that an un-simplified product rule expression may hide the correct answer.
in_depth.yield_rationale: MC answers are typically in simplified form; failure to simplify is a common source of answer-choice mismatches even when the rule is applied correctly.

in_depth.label: Extended Product Rule for Three Factors
in_depth.description: Differentiates a triple product f·g·h by applying the product rule twice: (fgh)' = f'gh + fg'h + fgh'; recognizes the pattern as a generalization of the two-factor rule.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Three-factor products appear rarely on the AB exam but do appear in MC when testing whether students can extend the product rule beyond two factors.

umbrellas.label: The Quotient Rule
umbrellas.description: Applies the quotient rule (f/g)' = (f'g − fg') / g² to differentiate a ratio of two differentiable functions (g ≠ 0); recognizes when a quotient structure requires this rule vs. rewriting as a product.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Quotient rule is heavily tested in MC; appears in implicit differentiation and related-rates setups that involve ratios; the numerator sign order is the highest-frequency error.

in_depth.label: Quotient Rule Setup and the Numerator Sign Order
in_depth.description: Sets up the quotient rule formula (f'g − fg') / g² with correct placement of f (numerator) and g (denominator); emphasizes that subtraction in the numerator is not commutative — f'g − fg' ≠ fg' − f'g.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Reversed numerator sign is the single most common quotient rule error and a standard distractor in MC; mastery of setup is the critical gate.

in_depth.label: Quotient Rule: Polynomial over Polynomial
in_depth.description: Differentiates a rational function (polynomial numerator divided by polynomial denominator) by applying the quotient rule and simplifying the resulting expression.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Poly-over-poly quotient rule problems are a standard MC type; requires polynomial algebra fluency for the numerator simplification step.

in_depth.label: Quotient Rule Involving Trigonometric Functions
in_depth.description: Applies the quotient rule to differentiate expressions like sin(x)/x, e^x/cos(x), or x/tan(x), combining trig derivatives with the quotient structure.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Trig-in-quotient problems are a frequent MC variant; they also provide the derivation route for sec(x), csc(x), tan(x), cot(x) derivatives covered in topic 2.10.

in_depth.label: Choosing Between Quotient Rule and Negative Exponent Rewrite
in_depth.description: Recognizes that 1/g(x) can be rewritten as g(x)^(−1) and differentiated via the power rule (or chain rule), whereas f(x)/g(x) requires the quotient rule; selects the efficient approach for a given expression.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Strategy selection between methods is tested in MC strategy questions; students who always use the quotient rule make avoidable algebraic errors on simple cases.

in_depth.label: Handling the g² Denominator in the Quotient Rule
in_depth.description: Correctly forms g(x)² in the denominator of the quotient rule result and leaves it un-expanded when appropriate; avoids erroneously canceling it with numerator terms.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Denominator errors (expanding g², incorrect squaring) are common algebraic mistakes that change the answer; tested in MC through answer-choice comparisons.

umbrellas.label: Derivatives of tan(x), cot(x), sec(x), and csc(x)
umbrellas.description: Applies the derived formulas d/dx(tan x) = sec²x, d/dx(cot x) = −csc²x, d/dx(sec x) = sec x tan x, d/dx(csc x) = −csc x cot x in problems involving these functions alone and combined with linearity rules.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: All six trig derivatives appear in MC; sec²(x) formula for tan'(x) is particularly common; these formulas are also used in trig-based quotient-rule problems and in integral forms (Unit 6).

in_depth.label: Derivative of tan(x) = sec²(x)
in_depth.description: Recalls d/dx(tan x) = sec²(x) and applies it to differentiate expressions involving tan(x), including with constant multiples or as part of a sum.
in_depth.yield_rationale: tan derivative is the most tested of the four remaining trig derivatives; sec²(x) appears in u-substitution integrals in Unit 6 and chain rule in Unit 3.

in_depth.label: Derivatives of sec(x) and csc(x)
in_depth.description: Recalls d/dx(sec x) = sec x tan x and d/dx(csc x) = −csc x cot x; notes the pattern that the derivative involves the same function times the co-function (with sign).
in_depth.yield_rationale: sec and csc derivatives appear in MC most years; the product-of-functions form makes them harder to memorize and more frequently missed.

in_depth.label: Derivative of cot(x) = −csc²(x)
in_depth.description: Recalls d/dx(cot x) = −csc²(x); distinguishes from d/dx(tan x) = sec²(x) by noting the negative sign and the cosecant-squared rather than secant-squared form.
in_depth.yield_rationale: cot derivative is the least commonly isolated but does appear in MC; confusion with the tan derivative formula is a common distractor.

in_depth.label: Deriving tan, cot, sec, csc Derivatives Using the Quotient Rule
in_depth.description: Derives d/dx(tan x) = sec²x by applying the quotient rule to sin x / cos x; similarly derives d/dx(sec x) by applying the quotient rule to 1/cos x; demonstrates that the four formulas follow from the sin/cos derivatives.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Derivation is occasionally tested in MC as 'which of the following is equivalent to the derivative of tan x found by the quotient rule'; confirms conceptual understanding over memorization.

in_depth.label: Differentiating Linear Combinations Involving All Six Trig Derivatives
in_depth.description: Applies sum/constant-multiple rules to differentiate expressions like f(x) = 2sec(x) − 5tan(x) + cot(x), requiring recall and correct application of all six trig derivative formulas simultaneously.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Multi-trig-function derivatives in a single expression appear in MC to test both formula fluency and linearity application.

umbrellas.label: Tangent Lines and Normal Lines
umbrellas.description: Finds the equation of the tangent line (or normal line) to a function at a given point by computing f'(a) and applying point-slope form; interprets the tangent line as the best linear approximation of f near x = a.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Tangent line equations are among the most common question types across Units 2-5 in both MC and FRQ; they appear explicitly or as a sub-part in related-rates, implicit differentiation, and linearization problems.

in_depth.label: Finding the Slope of the Tangent Line via f'(a)
in_depth.description: Computes f'(a) using the appropriate differentiation rule to obtain the slope of the tangent line at x = a; recognizes that f'(a) is the slope, not f(a).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Slope from derivative is the first step of every tangent-line sub-part; confusion between f(a) and f'(a) is the most common error in these problems.

in_depth.label: Writing the Tangent Line Equation Using Point-Slope Form
in_depth.description: Uses the point (a, f(a)) and slope m = f'(a) to write the tangent line equation y − f(a) = f'(a)(x − a), then simplifies to slope-intercept form when needed.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Writing the full tangent line equation is a standard FRQ sub-part; graders award process points for the correct point-slope setup even when arithmetic errors occur.

in_depth.label: Normal Line to a Curve (Perpendicular Slope)
in_depth.description: Finds the equation of the normal line at x = a by using the negative reciprocal of f'(a) as the slope, then applying point-slope form with the same point (a, f(a)).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Normal line questions appear in MC occasionally; less common than tangent line but frequently used as a distractor or alternative sub-part.

in_depth.label: Identifying the Tangent Line from a Graph (Slope Reading)
in_depth.description: Reads the slope of the tangent line at a marked point from a function graph by estimating the rise-over-run of the tangent, then writes or identifies the correct tangent line equation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graph-based tangent line slope reading connects graphical and analytic representations; appears in MC with graph stimuli (Part A no-calculator).

in_depth.label: Finding Points Where the Tangent Line is Horizontal
in_depth.description: Sets f'(x) = 0 and solves for x to locate points with a horizontal tangent (slope = 0); distinguishes these from vertical tangent points where f'(x) is undefined.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Horizontal tangent points are directly tested in MC and serve as the first step in critical-point analysis in Unit 5; high-frequency connection between Unit 2 and Unit 5.

umbrellas.label: Selecting and Combining Differentiation Rules
umbrellas.description: Identifies which differentiation rule(s) to apply when differentiating an expression that requires a combination of power, constant-multiple, product, quotient, and/or basic transcendental rules; performs the multi-step computation accurately.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Strategy-selection questions are a recurring MC type; combining multiple rules in a single expression is tested in FRQ on any differentiation sub-part.

in_depth.label: Distinguishing Product Structure from Composite Structure
in_depth.description: Given an expression, determines whether two functions are multiplied (requiring the product rule) or one is composed inside the other (requiring the chain rule from Unit 3); correctly categorizes before choosing the rule.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Product vs. composite misclassification is a major source of MC errors; this structural identification skill is the gateway to correct rule selection.

in_depth.label: Applying Product and Quotient Rules in a Single Expression
in_depth.description: Differentiates an expression that requires both the product and quotient rules — for example, a numerator that is itself a product — by applying the rules in the correct order.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Nested product-quotient structures appear in MC difficulty-escalation questions; tests procedural fluency and algebraic simplification simultaneously.

in_depth.label: Combining Power, Product/Quotient, and Transcendental Derivatives
in_depth.description: Differentiates expressions involving a product or quotient of a power function and a transcendental function (e.g., x²·sin x, e^x / x³), executing each sub-derivative and combining correctly.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Mixed-type derivatives are the dominant format for medium-to-hard MC derivative questions; these test simultaneous recall of multiple formula types.

in_depth.label: Simplifying the Expression Before Differentiating
in_depth.description: Recognizes when algebraic simplification (combining terms, canceling factors, distributing) before applying differentiation rules reduces computational complexity and avoids unnecessary use of the product or quotient rule.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Simplify-first strategy is tested in MC as the 'elegant' approach; recognizing the opportunity to simplify first is a speed and accuracy strategy on both sections.

in_depth.label: Computing the Second Derivative Using Basic Rules
in_depth.description: Differentiates f'(x) a second time using the same basic rules to obtain f''(x); applies the correct rule to each term in f'(x) including terms that involve products introduced by the product rule.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Second derivatives via basic rules appear in Unit 2 MC questions about concavity and in FRQ sub-parts before Unit 3 chain rule is needed; connects directly to Units 4 and 5.
